**Oslo Innovasjons program 2025**  
**Due midnight Monday Feb 3rd**

- Qs: 1 million total project or excluding self-funding?  **Total grant amount can be 1 million (total project 2 million)**  
- Qs: max 3 million per year for public funding  to a company (ask NFR)?

**Ideas:** 

* **Circular living lab \- Gamlebyen**:  
  * Hvordan kan gamlebyen loft bli sirkulær hub/verksted \- hjelpe andre huber eller nabolag å komme i gang   
  * Kunnskapshus med kurs og kompetanseutvikling   
  * Oslo Circular living lab \- avansert kunnskap, deler det, prøver å konvertere til konkrete løsninger. Skille oss fra Økern   
  * Mat og tekstil \- knytter oss til kunnskapsmiljøer rundt dette  
  * Materialstrøm på klær og mat kan være en mulighet   
  * Northern Playground \- holde kurs om hvilke koder de har knekt   
  * Hvem er målgruppen \- voksne, håndarbeid   
  * Undervurderte ferdigheter \- som håndarbeid   
  * Tekstil og mat, kortlevd og   
  * Systemfastlåsning og regimer, lineært  
  * De som produserer og serverer \- øke lokalmatproduksjon   
  * Sustainable food systems   
  * Gamlebyen mat og drikke kan lære bort teknikker (?)  
  * Lage tekstiler av matavfall \- eksempel fra Brasil  
  * Hvor mange koblinger er det mellom tekstil og mat  
  * Kaffeposer, frakt og emballasje  
  * **Mat, tekstil og emballasje**  
  * Jobbe med andre bransjer/sektorer \- drikkekartong kan brukes til å lage byggeplater til byggindustrien   
  * Converting knowledge to practical solutions \- innføring i sirkulæritet på overordnet nivå \- 3 fragmenter med spesifikke målsettinger/legislations   
  * Kartlegge mulige deltagere \- nettverket  
  * Arbeidsplasser gjennom sirkulære forretningsmodeller i Gamlebyen  
  * Oppstartssentral \-   
  * Tjenestelabratorium \- leasingløsning  
  * Reparasjon, vedlikehold   
  * Begynne med Gamlebyen \- et sted vi har troverdighet   
  * HORECA hotell, restaurant og catering \- kompost, dyrefor eller redusere matavfall  
  * I gamlebyen skal man bidra til en reparasjonskultur  
  * I gamlebyen skal man bidra til å redusere matavfallet   
  * Potensielle samarbeidspartnere Bjørvika bydelslag/velforening   
  * Bydelsutvalget \- Agnes   
  * Yrkesskole på Økern, sømlinje og kokkelinje  
  * Kokk og servitørfag, Etterstad videregående skole   
  * Sirkulæritet inn i yrkesopplæringen   
  * Image academy Akersgata  
  * Fæbric  
  * Northern Playground  
  * NFTEA \- klynge for tekstilnæring i Norge   
  * Synergier   
  * Jobbe med andre bransjer/sektorer \- drikkekartong kan brukes til å lage byggeplater til byggindustrien   
  * Converting knowledge to practical solutions \- innføring i sirkulæritet på overordnet nivå \- 3 fragmenter med spesifikke målsettinger/legislations   
  * **Mappe de aktørene som allerede finnes i gamlebyen \- lokal verdiskapning**   
  * **Teste vellykkede europeiske insentiver \- reparasjon og vedlikeholdstilskudd, alle kommer til gamlebyen for å fikse klær og sko \- skape lokale arbeidsplasser, redusere avfall**

### Background info from Ceclie

The food system in Oslo has not prioritised waste as a resource, but rather approaches waste in a more traditionally circular way of thinking–focusing on recycling such as with plastic bottles and paper in norway. The problem when you transfer this mindset to food you loose a lot of potential because the complexity is so much bigger and also the potential benefit an value, but it requires a full “cradle to cradle” mindset in how we design our food system into a circular model. Production of soil and especially foodsoils is one of the big solutions to the climate crisis, and the fact that soil binds co2 when you increase the nourishment is something that very few climate activists take into their programs. 

There was a soil health course building on regenerative farming principles in Fokhol in 2017 initiated by biodynamisk assosiation with Friedrich Wenz ([Humus Farming/Wenz Academy](http://www.humusfarming.de/)), Dietmar Näser ([Grüne Brücke](http://www.gruenebruecke.de/)) og Martin Beck ([Almende ApS](https://almende.dk/)). One of the projects coming out from this was led by Andreas Capjon cityfarmer of Oslo at that time located in Losæter. He experimented with a lot of small circle loop systems, picking up food waste at the michelin star restaurant Maaemo in the neighbourhood (where he knew all the produce were biodynamic and organic) and composting it back to soil at Losæter. He used bokashi in the first years and did not have any issues with rats even though he worked in open air systems. Today he has expanded this learning into Ureist and has now started working on bigger composting machines for bigger scale kitchens. 

One of the local forces who have focused on making small circle loops in Oslo is Siri Mittet from Gruten. She has collected coffee grut from local coffee shops to make soap and oyster mushrooms. In Australia a player like Matt & Lentil from Grown & Gathered, did a similar small-scale project collecting coffee leftovers and food waste from restaurants, making compost at their farm and delivering flowers and vegetables back to the restaurants. They even did one compost on only coffee leftovers that was working really well.

If you look at the ideal circle loop, where you have a cradle to cradle mindset \- the biodynamic farmers or regenerative farms are light houses in showing systems where you have a closed circular loop where nothing outside the farm goes in. In Gamlebyen today this must be addressed at what we buy into our food channels. What we buy in is important because by supporting the producers who don't use glyphosate, hormones and antibiotic in their production you support the whole eco system with soil health and water system health: The oslo fjord region is a very good example on this because the problem in the Oslo fjord is now so big, and they have addressed that the farmers are one of the main causes of the imbalance in the eco system. Players like Tore Jardar from Virgernes farm have with his regenerative practice shown how you can reverse this and really repair what we have destroyed. On the other side very few kitchens in the Oslo region support these type of farmers, you have of course the light house players like Esben from Maaemo, Heidi Bjerkan in Nasjonalmuseet, Veslemøy Hvidsten at Nektar leading the way, but to get here there is a long way for our lunch routine at Gamlebyen loft and the catering business Gamlebyen Mat og Drikke.

When the food waste consists of traces from glyphosate, antibiotics and other things you would not like in a compost if the soil is going to be used to produce food. The Oslo compost (soil production is oslo) is known by biologists working on soil health to be “dead”. So creating something that looks like soil, and what is a healthy soil binding co2 with nourishing humus is two very different things. If we really want to change the system we have to go through the whole system with all the steps in the loop. This might have to be a step by step process because it creates a change in mindset. If you truly want to create a sustainable menu (like BERAS project [diet for a green planet](https://dietforagreenplanet.se/en/projects/beras/) in Sweden have focused on), you can´t just replace one product with the other. You need to shift the mindset of planning the menu after the season, using preserving techniques and having storage. You need to go down on meat and rather use broth and think of meat as a spice rather than a dominant part of a plate. In Norway Matvalget has been the one implementing this mindset into Norwegian cantines and helping them change step by step.  

* Materialstrømmer   
  * Materialstrømmer og verdikjeder \- hente inn Maren?   
  * Modellutvikling bli et nytt produkt, en ny tjeneste, en ny bedrift   
  * Tjenestemodeller og løsninger


* Lokal mat  
* Share knowledge

### Application background docs

[https://www.oslo.kommune.no/getfile.php/13526499-1733922800/Tjenester%20og%20tilbud/Tilskudd%2C%20legater%20og%20stipend/Innovasjonsprogram%20Oslo/Retningslinjer%20for%20Innovasjonsprogram%20Oslo%202025.pdf](https://www.oslo.kommune.no/getfile.php/13526499-1733922800/Tjenester%20og%20tilbud/Tilskudd%2C%20legater%20og%20stipend/Innovasjonsprogram%20Oslo/Retningslinjer%20for%20Innovasjonsprogram%20Oslo%202025.pdf)

[https://www.oslo.kommune.no/getfile.php/13387560-1638265512/Tjenester%20og%20tilbud/Tilskudd%2C%20legater%20og%20stipend/Innovasjonsprogram%20Oslo/Regional%20plan%20for%20innovasjon%20og%20nyskaping%20i%20Oslo%20og%20Akershus%20fram%20mot%202025.pdf](https://www.oslo.kommune.no/getfile.php/13387560-1638265512/Tjenester%20og%20tilbud/Tilskudd%2C%20legater%20og%20stipend/Innovasjonsprogram%20Oslo/Regional%20plan%20for%20innovasjon%20og%20nyskaping%20i%20Oslo%20og%20Akershus%20fram%20mot%202025.pdf)

**2\. Objectives for the Grant Scheme**

**Purpose of the program**  
The Innovation Program Oslo aims to contribute to:

* Strengthening and developing the innovation system in Oslo through innovation and collaboration between businesses, knowledge environments, public institutions, entrepreneurs, and capital environments.  
* Improving the international competitiveness of businesses in Oslo.  
* The creation of new jobs and scaling of start-up companies.

Through the program, funds are allocated to projects that contribute to solving the most important business policy challenges for Oslo, as indicated in the Regional Plan for Innovation and New Business Development in Oslo and Akershus until 2025\.

**Challenges**  
The plan highlights five specific challenges and focus areas that the Innovation Program Oslo aims to address:

* **Cluster and network development**: Strengthening knowledge transfer and innovation within and across clusters, business networks, incubators, and innovation districts.  
* **Entrepreneurship and growth businesses**: Enhanced efforts for the establishment of new businesses with growth opportunities in an international market.  
* **Supplier development and service innovation**: Developing more and better meeting places between public purchasers and the supplier market for implementing innovative public procurement.  
* **Early-stage financing**: Improved access to risk capital for knowledge-based companies in the early stages.  
* **Commercialization of research results**: Developing arenas, networks, and programs between research environments and companies, ensuring that the commercialization of research results leads to the creation of new businesses.

**3\. Award Criteria**  
Projects will be assessed based on how well the application meets the following criteria: a. It contributes to solving one or more of the five themes listed above, as described in the Regional Plan for Innovation and New Business Development in Oslo and Akershus until 2025\. b. **It contributes to green and sustainable transformation and innovation.** c. **It demonstrates collaboration between several of the following actor groups: public sector, businesses, academia, and entrepreneurship and capital environments.** d. The support from the program has a triggering effect, meaning the support should significantly contribute to the initiation/implementation of the project (it is not sufficient for the grant to merely support maintaining an existing offering). e. The project appears feasible within the specified project period.

**4\. Target Group and Who Can Apply for the Program**  
Funds from the program are primarily intended to benefit small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in Oslo, through collaboration on innovation activities aimed at increasing innovation, development, and value creation.

The following entities can apply for the program:

* Clusters, networks, incubators, and entrepreneurial environments.  
* Research institutes, educational institutions, and knowledge providers.  
* Innovation environments/companies in collaboration with businesses.  
* Companies on behalf of a network or collaborative constellation.

. **Indikatorer** som brukes er: ▪ Antall nye bedriftsetableringer ▪ Antall bedrifter som deltar i prosjektet ▪ Antall nye arbeidsplasser som skapes gjennom prosjektet ▪ Antall nye produkter og prosesser som er innført hos deltagende bedrifter/ og eller i markedet ▪ Antall patentsøknader og/ eller lisenser ▪ Andel privat finansiering som tildelingen utløser ▪ Internasjonale prosjekter utløst som resultat av prosjekt

# Two Applications

1. ## Fagmiljø og entreprenørskaps arena for innovative urbane matsystemet for Oslo regionen er etablert lokalt i St. Halvards gate 33

### Short description (500 tegn)

### Project description (8000)

“Few things are as interwoven with human existence, culture, and the economy as food. At the most basic level, we need food to survive. Beyond sustenance, food can bring joy and has a central role in cultures around the world, often as the centrepiece of celebrations and festivities. The global food industry is also the world’s largest sector, employing over 1 billion people and accounting for around 10% of global GDP.[^1]”

Ours will be a preliminary project

### Bakgrunn (8000 tegn- ca 2 pages)- 

*Describe the background of the project and why it is necessary to initiate it. The background should explain why this specific project needs to be carried out. The application should have a clear and consistent narrative where the background/problem statement, goals/sub-goals, method, and activity plan align with each other. Also, indicate whether this project is part of a larger project or if it is a preliminary/main project\!*

**The problem:** Modern food systems rely heavily on finite resources, generate excessive waste, and contribute to environmental pollution. Within these linear food systems, large amounts of phosphorus, potassium, and other non-renewable materials are mined for farming, while six garbage trucks' worth of edible food is wasted every second—yet less than 2% of the nutrients from discarded organics are recovered. Additionally, pesticides and synthetic fertilizers contaminate soil and water, while mismanaged food waste and byproducts further degrade soil and environmental ecosystems.**[^2]**

**Circular economy as a solution:** Circular economy principles focus on designing out waste and pollution, keeping products and materials in use, and regenerating natural systems.  A circular food system model operates through a biological materials cycle: where food and natural materials in packaging like cotton, jute, or wood are designed to return to the system via composting or anaerobic digestion. These processes regenerate ecosystems, such as soil, which sustain renewable resources. A circular food system can thus mimic natural regeneration, eliminating waste by turning it into feedstock for new cycles. Contaminant-free organic by-products can safely return to the soil as fertilizer, enhancing sustainability. When done regeneratively, this approach offers multiple benefits, including:

- Enhanced green spaces in cities  
- Stronger community engagement  
- Reconnection with food production  
- Increased urban carbon sequestration  
- Improved air quality and heat mitigation  
- Better soil water retention, reducing flood risks

**Why focus on urban food systems?** With nearly 80% of the world’s food consumed in cities, urban food systems play a crucial role in global sustainability. As urban populations grow, cities face unique challenges due to their complexity, diverse communities, and reliance on external food supplies. Unlike rural areas, cities have greater political agility to drive change but are also more vulnerable to supply chain disruptions that affect food security, as seen during the COVID-19 pandemic. Strengthening urban food systems is essential for resilience and food security.[^3]

**Why focus on businesses?**  

- **Waste systems:** The current food waste systems in Oslo are focused on the public sector, with no obligations for the commercial sector on waste.  However, we are seeing regulatory shifts and there is a growing expectation that regulations on commercial waste are coming, necessitating a clear understanding based on practical experience from the business community of how to address waste streams.  
- **Fragmented systems:** Oslo’s food system is highly fragmented, where the essential role of food in a resilient community is often overlooked. Despite food being central to daily life, it lacks a cohesive structure. This fragmentation presents opportunities to rethink local sourcing and waste management, such as repurposing coffee industry byproducts or making better use of leftover food from catering for free lunches for the surrounding businesses. Businesses are a perfect opportunity for finding ways to reuse waste as resources and collaborate in hyper local contexts for reducing costs.  
- **Business development districts or placemaking projects present key opportunities for better collaborative planning:** When planning a new development area, business and organizational tenants are often put together with no foresight on potential synergies.  Even in state-of-the-art strategic planning processes–where we strategically choose businesses that have the potential to lift an area up–often lack foresight or understanding of how businesses could collaborate on resource and waste streams for mutual benefits.  Working practically with businesses on circular food systems would enable us to innovatively and strategically plan new development areas where different actors are programmed into a building or district to create synergies.

Current best practices in circular resource usage in the food system that do exist are based on siloed systems, creating the following problems:

1. **Siloed sectors:** There is little cross-sectoral cooperation on waste streams between the different sectors in the food industry value chain.  For example, there are very few connections between urban producers, the textiles and packaging sectors, the restaurant industry, and soil production.  This lack of cooperation and competence sharing leads to the loss of opportunities for reusing waste streams as resources.

2. **Siloed resource use:** There is little understanding of how materials within the waste streams are usable as resources.  For example, composting food waste is often proposed as a viable environmental sustainability strategy, but the regulations on using food waste for soil to grow food for commercial usage create strong barriers to the uptake of this as a circular solution.  This lack of understanding of the variety of materials in waste streams and how they could be grouped according to regulatory hindrances to circularity leads to a lack of investment and experimentation with viable circular solutions.

**Best practice cases in Oslo area:**

1. **Losæter and Small-Scale Circular Systems:** In 2017, a soil health course inspired by regenerative farming principles was held at Fokhol, initiated by the Biodynamic Association and led by experts Friedrich Wenz, Dietmar Näser, and Martin Beck. One notable outcome was the work of Andreas Capjon, then Oslo’s city farmer based at Losæter. Capjon implemented small-loop systems by collecting food waste from Michelin-starred restaurant Maaemo, where ingredients were biodynamic and organic, and composting it back into soil. Using bokashi composting methods, Capjon avoided issues such as pests despite operating in open-air systems. He has since expanded these learnings through the business Ureist, which uses urban spaces that are set to be developed as small urban farms with on-site composting facilities.  
2. **Gruten: Repurposing Coffee Waste:** Another Oslo-based initiative, Gruten, led by Siri Mittet, demonstrated the potential of small-scale circular loops. Gruten collected coffee grounds from local cafes to produce soap and cultivate oyster mushrooms. Internationally, similar efforts, such as Matt and Lentil’s project in Australia, have shown how food waste from restaurants can be transformed into compost, used to grow vegetables and flowers, which are then returned to the restaurants.  
3. **Fuglen/Gamlebyen Loft??:** Connections with Albildsøe gård for coffee grounds composting, …. food and beverage industry tenants of the Gamlebyen Loft area have been at the leading edge of their industries in Oslo, and are leading a culture of sustainability in their respective business communities???? Fuglen…  Gamlebyen Mat og Drikke??.  
4. **NMBU Arena for Sustainable Food Systems:** Like many knowledge-institution based arenas, NMBU’s centre focuses on the connections between rural food production and urban distribution and consumption.  It also lacks a business focus, emphasizing research, education, and communication of food systems.  The arena also focuses more narrowly on food waste, rather than all of the potential circular products within the food system value chain (such as packaging waste, textiles, etc).  
5. **EAT forum:** EAT forum aims to catalyze a food system transformation by connecting partners across science, business, policy, and civil society.  Their aim is a sustainable global food system, and they work towards this goal through communication of knowledge and networking activities.  EAT’s sustainability focus however, is on food from a human health perspective–healthy diets for a healthy planet–rather than on broader circular transitions.

**Creating an arena for circular local food systems:**   
The problems of siloed working and siloed resources highlights the need for: 1\. Practical knowledge in circular food systems for the business sector, where businesses are invested in trialling solutions up and down the value chains to understand how systems can operate together; and 2\. A local urban food systems competence sharing arena–where complex issues are simplified into practical knowledge that businesses can easily adopt.

**Why Gamlebyen?** Gamlebyen Loft in Bydel Gamle Oslo is a hub for innovation and collaboration, which since 2018 has brought businesses together to create a sustainable and thriving local community. In 2021 and again in 2024 new building premises were added to the business community, with 33 businesses/organizations operating in the Loft area with approximately 150 employees, and 15 new studio spaces available in the summer of 2025\.  Operated by Natural State AS, the Gamlebyen Loft business and innovation community works at the forefront of environmental sustainability, as demonstrated by projects like Norway's first use of recycled brick facades. We will also soon welcome the Arne Næss Centre for Ecology and Philosophy (ANCEP) into the Gamlebyen Loft community–deepening the connections between the academic philosophy of ecological sustainability and practical action in the business community.  The Loft currently serves as a key networking point, fostering connections between businesses, non-profit organizations, and the local community. With events like the Christmas market, open summer fest, monthly ‘Pils & PowerPoint’ Fridays, community use of the lofthall, and initiatives to integrate new tenants, we enable both professional growth and community engagement. 

### Project Goals (8000)- Objectives 

*The project goal should describe what you aim to achieve with this specific project. The project should have concrete, realistic, and measurable goals/sub-goals. Additionally, explain how you will determine whether the goals are met. Furthermore, you must describe how achieving the goals of this project contributes to achieving the objectives of the funding scheme.*

**Introduction: Challenges and Opportunities**:  
Currently, few Oslo-based food and beverage industry businesses have cross-sectoral connections with businesses outside of the food industry. While pioneers such as Maaemo, Nasjonalmuseet’s Heidi Bjerkan, and Veslemøy Hvidsten at Nektar lead by example, there is a significant gap in integrating these principles into everyday food operations, and the connections  that do exist are in the ‘farm-to-fork’ sector–between high-end restaurants and farms.  There are few connections with high quality soil producers, and even fewer with businesses in the packaging and textile waste streams.   There are critical barriers to reuse of waste streams as resources outside of the ‘farm-to-fork’ model, due to a lack of general overview of which waste streams lack regulatory hindrances to usage and which waste streams have hindrances from effects further down in the circular system.  For example, using vegetable food waste to make soil through composting can create negative effects from contamination of food waste with glyphosate (pesticide) residues, antibiotics, and other harmful substances which compromise the quality of compost. Biologists working on soil health have for example, criticized Oslo’s current compost as "dead," underscoring the need for a systemic understanding of which waste streams could more easily be taken up into circular systems.  Regenerating soil to be used in and around the businesses in Gamlebyen that trial these solutions creates habitats for local biodiversity.  This soil could thus be used for growing plants, but also in pollinator passageways for local honey production, as well as providing local ecosystem services such as mitigating flooding effects through water retention.

The **Food Arena for Circular Urban Food Systems** project aims to position Oslo as a leader in circular food systems by identifying, investing in trialling, and broadly communicating innovative and measurable solutions to transition from a linear to a circular food industry. The project focuses on promoting circular culture, fostering collaboration, and addressing barriers to circularity. Based on the identified challenges of **1\. Siloed working; and 2\. Siloed waste streams,** this pre-project will thus identify the opportunities and barriers to circularity in the local context within the food and beverage industry, trial hyper local solutions together with local businesses, and create a networking and competence building arena for circularity in the food and beverage industry.  The goals of this arena will be: 

1. **Understand the potential for Circular Food System Solutions in Oslo’s food sector business community:**  
2. **Develop and test regenerative food system solutions in partnership with local businesses to identify scalable strategies:**  
3. **Establish Oslo’s Urban Food System Competence Hub**

#### Goals with Sub-Goals:

1. **Understand the potential for Circular Food System Solutions in Oslo’s food sector business community:**  
   * **Sub-goal 1.1: Mapping Resources and Barriers:** Conduct a comprehensive mapping of local food industry resources, waste streams, barriers to circularity, and global best practices.  
     * **Milestone:** Complete and publish one mapping report by summer 2025\.  
   * **Sub-goal 1.2: Knowledge sharing dialogues:** Host five networking workshops with knowledge sharing dialogues with Oslo businesses, knowledge institutions (SINTEF, OsloMet), regulatory bodies, and key knowledge communities (Nordic Circular Hotspot), to present the findings of the mapping report and provide actionable insights on implementing circular solutions.  Experience-sharing among actors will help address methodological and practical challenges more easily, and enable us to uncover untapped circular resources for future start-up business development.  
     * **Milestone:** Completion of the workshops and engagement of at least 35 participating businesses.  
2. **Develop and test regenerative food system solutions in partnership with local businesses to identify scalable strategies:**  
   * Sub-goal 1.1: Pilot Implementation of Regenerative Practices  
     * Collaborate with at least three local businesses to trial regenerative food system solutions, such as sustainable sourcing, composting, and closed-loop supply chains. Document best practices and challenges to inform future scalability.  
     * Milestone: At least three businesses successfully integrate regenerative practices and demonstrate measurable improvements in sustainability metrics by 2026\.  
   * Sub-goal 1.2: Impact Assessment and Reporting  
     * Conduct an evaluation of the economic, environmental, and social impacts of the implemented regenerative food solutions, comparing pre- and post-trial data.  
     * Milestone: Publish a comprehensive impact report by the end of 2025, outlining key findings, challenges, and recommendations for scaling up solutions.  
3. **Establish Oslo’s Urban Food System Competence Hub**  
   * Sub-goal 2.1: Knowledge Sharing Newsletter  
     * Launch a quarterly newsletter to disseminate insights, case studies, and updates on urban food system innovation, connecting businesses, policymakers, and researchers.  
     * Milestone: Publish at least three editions of the newsletter by 2025, reaching a minimum of 200 subscribers.  
   * Sub-goal 2.2: Stakeholder Seminars  
     * Organize four seminars focused on urban food system innovation, bringing together key stakeholders to foster collaboration and share best practices.  
     * Milestone: Successfully host four seminars by 2026, engaging at least 100 participants in total.  
   * Sub-goal 2.3: Community Engagement and Network Expansion  
     * Strengthen Oslo’s urban food ecosystem by collaborating with key local actors such as ByBi, Losæter, Fuglen, Food Studio, and Abildsø Farm. Facilitate cross-sector partnerships to drive innovation and practical solutions.  
     * Milestone: Establish at least five new collaboration partnerships by 2026\.

#### How these goals are related to the programme’s priorities:

### Forankring (context)- (8000)- Oslo

For example, if physical investments are to be made, specify whether these are in accordance with the zoning plan.*

* Talk about how festivals/events are perfect closed loop systems to identify circular waste stream opportunities since the consumer waste is disposed of at the same site as purchasing.   
* Talk about the social environment of cooperation that exists (Gamlebyen Mat og Drikke leftovers, Pils\&Powerpoint (One Friday a month) as a co-learning environment.

Additionally, describe any other human resources (competence, knowledge, skills, networks, etc.) intended to be utilized in the project to support its implementation and achievement of goals.*

### Partners  

*Søker skal vise hvem man samarbeider med, hvordan partene bidrar til prosjektet og hvilke andre finansieringsmuligheter som er vurdert.*

Vi arbeider tett med et bredt spekter av selskaper, organisasjoner og med Oslo kommune i flere av våre prosjekter. I våre viktigste samarbeid har vi landets største og minste bedrifter, samt ved inkludering ved unge er det det den unge som selv ønsker å lære.  
Alle de ovennevnte selskapene er med i fellesskapet Gamlebyen Loft eller har produksjonsbedrifter i St. Halvardsgate 33 som er eiendommen. og bidrar til et aktivt og innovativt miljø. Det er ikke medtatt navn på privatpersoner fra nærmiljøet som vi samarbeider med av hensyn til personvern.

NCH  
Local businesses  
Bybi  
SINTEF  
Ureist Andreas Capjon  
Linda Jolly former NMBU biologist and soil health.   
Troels Fuglsang Sperlebakken / Julius Maske og Synne Refnes Storbuan / Elisabeth Fagerland \- rolemodels

Losæter ny bybonde Kia Vulgaris (mammaperm?)  
Hafdís Sunna Hermannsdóttir Dagens  
Svein Kjøde phd på systemendring i bærekraft og styremeldem Dagens (https://www.kreativtforum.no/artikler/overganger/svein-kjode-returnerer-til-aera-med-doktorgrad)

### Activities: how to achieve the outputs and objectives  

*Describe the activities to be carried out during the project period, i.e., what will specifically be done with the funds allocated to the project from various financing sources. The activities undertaken should be rooted in the background and problem statement and should be reflected in the cost estimate.*

![A diagram of a diagramDescription automatically generated][image1]

### **Goal 1: Mapping the Potential for Circular Food System Solutions**

#### **Sub-goal 1.1: Mapping Resources and Barriers**

**Activities:**

1. **Mapping of Local Resources, Waste Streams, and Stakeholders:**  
   * Conduct a detailed mapping of inputs and outputs (potential waste streams) for events, businesses, and the food industry.  
   * Identify and categorize key local stakeholders, including businesses, regulatory bodies, and NGOs, involved in Oslo’s food system.  
   * Adapt tools like **smart journey mining** to track waste streams in restaurants and cafés, using automated data capture and predictive analytics.  
   * Identify inefficiencies (e.g., recyclables in general waste) and opportunities for collaboration, such as connecting surplus food to donation programs or coffee grounds to local farms.  
2. **Barriers to Circular Practices:**  
   * Identify and document barriers to circular practices in Oslo's food system, such as financial, logistical, and regulatory challenges.  
   * Gather input from stakeholders to uncover pain points and challenges faced when adopting circular solutions.  
3. **Mapping Best Practices:**  
   * Research and compile global and European best practices for circular solutions, focusing on innovations relevant to Oslo’s food system.  
   * Highlight examples of successful circular practices in waste reduction, resource recovery, and value chain optimization.  
4. **Report Preparation:**  
   * Synthesize findings into a comprehensive mapping report, including actionable recommendations, opportunities, and frameworks for circular solutions in Oslo’s food industry.

**Measurement:**

* Completion and publication of the mapping report by summer 2025\.

---

#### **Sub-goal 1.2: Knowledge Sharing Dialogues**

**Activities:**

1\) helping our target stakeholders understand the latest science on healthy and sustainable food systems, and 2\) mobilizing our network of experts to provide the technical support needed to concretize actions – including helping to set science-based targets (SBTs) for urban food systems, grounded in policy and operationalized in interventions. To do this, we will work with city officials (to understand the local context and priorities and identify relevant data) and the research community (to conduct the technical work and data analysis). Other potential actors that would be relevant to this work include businesses and community leaders on the ground, who can help gather local data and knowledge.

1. **Workshops with Cross-Sectoral Core Groups:**  
   * Identify and convene a cross-sectoral group of stakeholders, including established businesses, start-ups, and actors from different parts of the value chain.  
   * Organize five workshops focused on knowledge-sharing, solutions co-creation, and identifying incentives for circular practices.  
2. **Networking and Study Trips:**  
   * Facilitate **chef and farmer networking sessions**, where chefs visit farms and/or soil production facilities and vice versa to foster collaboration and circular thinking.  
   * Organize study trips to showcase best practices and foster cross-sectoral learning.  
3. **Workshop Design and Execution:**  
   * Develop workshop agendas around key topics such as optimizing waste streams, creating circular incentives, and addressing regulatory barriers.  
   * Host interactive sessions with expert presentations, case studies, and collaborative problem-solving exercises.  
4. **Follow-Up and Documentation:**  
   * Document workshop outcomes, including participant feedback, insights, and actionable steps.  
   * Share findings with the broader stakeholder group to maintain momentum and foster collaboration.

**Measurement:**

* Completion of five workshops with engagement of at least 35 participating businesses.

---

### **Goal 2: Establish Oslo’s Urban Food System Competence Hub**

#### Broker interaction between stakeholders, sparking debate and enabling collaboration that can spur into action. • Harness opportunities to promote urban-focused content in our communications and events, under the narrative of “cities as agents and arenas for change for achieving global food systems transformation”. • Use high-profile events and other platforms to place a spotlight on local leadership (including government, business, and civil society) and generate endorsement and actions from the highest level of food systems governance.

#### **Sub-goal 2.1: Newsletter**

**Activities:**

1. **Content Development:**  
   * Develop engaging and informative content on circular food system innovations, mapping findings, and global best practices.  
   * Highlight success stories and updates from workshops, study trips, and mapping activities.  
2. **Distribution and Engagement:**  
   * Build and maintain a subscriber base of stakeholders, including local businesses, NGOs, and the wider Oslo community.  
   * Distribute newsletters regularly, emphasizing actionable insights and opportunities for collaboration.

**Measurement:**

* Ongoing engagement with newsletter subscribers.

---

#### **Sub-goal 2.2: Seminars**

**Activities:**

1. **Seminar Planning and Organization:**  
   * Organize four seminars to promote urban food system innovation and share circular solutions.  
   * Focus themes on innovative waste stream integration, circular business models, and policy frameworks.  
2. **Speaker and Participant Engagement:**  
   * Invite industry leaders, policymakers, and local businesses to present best practices and discuss challenges.  
   * Promote seminars to attract a diverse and engaged audience from across Oslo.  
3. **Execution and Documentation:**  
   * Facilitate interactive discussions and networking opportunities during the seminars.  
   * Document and share key takeaways, insights, and recommendations with stakeholders.

**Measurement:**

* Host four seminars by 2026, attracting at least 100 participants in total.

---

#### **Sub-goal 2.3: Community Engagement**

**Activities:**

1. **Ecosystem Mapping and Outreach:**  
   * Identify and engage key community stakeholders, such as ByBi, Losæter, Fuglen, Food Studio, and Abildsø Farm.  
   * Establish collaborations with at least five new partners to strengthen the local ecosystem.  
2. **Community Events and Pilot Projects:**  
   * Organize events to foster circular culture and showcase innovative circular solutions.  
   * Collaborate with community partners on pilot projects, such as repurposing food waste for animal feed or packaging for construction materials.  
3. **Capacity Building:**  
   * Provide training and resources to local stakeholders to strengthen their understanding of circular principles.  
   * Facilitate dialogues between community actors to encourage cross-sectoral collaboration.

**Measurement:**

* Engagement of at least five new collaboration partners.

---

### **Evaluation and Monitoring Activities**

**Activities:**

1. **Tracking Metrics:**  
   * Monitor attendance and engagement at workshops, seminars, and other events.  
   * Track progress on mapping reports and community engagement targets.  
2. **Stakeholder Feedback:**  
   * Collect feedback from participants to assess the impact and effectiveness of activities.  
   * Use feedback to refine future activities and improve stakeholder engagement.  
3. **Impact Assessment:**  
   * Evaluate the project’s contributions to fostering circularity in Oslo’s food and beverage industry.  
   * Document success stories, lessons learned, and opportunities for scaling successful practices.

**Ideas for activities:**  
Core living lab:

1. Map an event and all the inputs and outputs in terms of waste streams (not a MFA, just the potential waste streams?)  Map all the stakeholders and current best practices for both local businesses and best practices globally (what exists, what is needed).  
2. Network building and Communication strategy with workshops/seminars:  Identify a cross-sectoral core group which includes both established businesses and start-ups, as well as businesses from different parts of the value chain, to experiment with solutions and incentives.  Including study trips; chef & farmer networking where the chefs visit the farmers and the farmer visit the chefs.

**1\. Mapping of Local Resources, Waste, Barriers, and Knowledge**  
**Activity 1.1:** Conduct a detailed mapping of local resources (e.g., food waste, by-products, non-food waste such as packaging, etc) and waste streams within Oslo's food industry. Identify key local stakeholders and businesses involved.  Smart journey mining could be adapted to optimize waste streams in the restaurant and food sector, addressing not only food waste but also packaging, recyclables, and general waste. By using automated data capture, a café could for example log information about waste types, amounts, and the times they are generated, much like tracking customer journeys. Machine learning and logic-based tools could then identify inefficiencies—such as recyclable materials ending up in general waste—or opportunities to redirect resources like surplus food to donation programs, composting facilities, or animal feed. Predictive analytics could forecast future waste trends, allowing the café to plan partnerships with businesses that are interested in the waste as a resource.  The tool could facilitate collaboration by matching waste outputs with other businesses’ needs, such as connecting used coffee grounds to local farms or packaging to a start-up making building materials with plastic waste.  Successful adoption will necessitate close collaboration with businesses in the early stages with a willingness to share waste-related data and address logistical challenges such as storage and compliance with safety regulations.   
**Activity 1.2:** Identify barriers to the uptake of the identified resources in terms of circular practices in Oslo's food system, including financial, logistical, and regulatory challenges.  
**Activity 1.3:** Investigate and compile best practices for circular solutions from Europe and other global regions, focusing on those relevant to Oslo's food system as defined in activity 1.1.

**2\. Communication and Knowledge Dissemination Strategy**  
Activity 2.1: Develop a communication strategy tailored to local food businesses to promote awareness and engagement with circular solutions.  
Activity 2.2: Organize a series of workshops with local businesses to disseminate knowledge from the mapping reports and share circular economy practices.

### Target groups 

*Describe who the project is intended to reach. The target groups may include, for example, businesses, the general population, or specific segments of the population (based on gender, age, background). Explain how the target group(s) will be involved in the project.*

Local businesses  
Local consumers/residents  
Bydel Gamle Oslo er hovedmålgruppen vår i tillegg til de som jobber i en av bedriftene i Gamlebyen Loft-fellesskapet. Vi retter oss direkte mot mindre selskaper som har en etablert kundebase, men som fremdeles ikke har et fast fotfeste i sitt marked.

### Results: concrete outputs 

*Based on the information that I will provide below, can you please make some results for this project using the following criteria: The project will involve a range of activities, as previously described. Here, detail the specific results you expect these activities to yield. The results should be described clearly, be measurable, and verifiable.*  
*Here is the data to use to define the results:*

### **Results for the Circular Food System Solutions Project**

#### **Goal 1: Mapping the Potential for Circular Food System Solutions**

**Sub-goal 1.1: Mapping Resources and Barriers** **Results:**

1. **Comprehensive Mapping Report:**  
   * Detailed mapping of at least 50 food industry stakeholders, including businesses, regulatory bodies, NGOs, and knowledge institutions, categorized by role and potential contributions to a circular food system.  
   * Identification and documentation of 20+ key waste streams, inefficiencies, and opportunities for resource recovery (e.g., connecting coffee grounds to farms or surplus food to donation programs).  
   * Mapping of at least 10 financial, logistical, and regulatory barriers to circularity in Oslo’s food system.  
   * Compilation of 15+ global and European best practices for circular solutions, contextualized for Oslo’s food industry.  
2. **Actionable Recommendations:**  
   * A prioritized list of opportunities, challenges, and frameworks for implementing circular food solutions.  
   * A roadmap for businesses to address inefficiencies, improve resource recovery, and adopt innovative waste management practices.

**Verification:**

* The report will be publicly available by summer 2025, with measurable deliverables (number of stakeholders mapped, waste streams analyzed, and best practices compiled).  
* Stakeholder feedback collected during report dissemination will validate its relevance and accuracy.

---

**Sub-goal 1.2: Knowledge Sharing Dialogues** **Results:**

1. **Workshops with Cross-Sectoral Core Groups:**  
   * Completion of five interactive workshops by the end of 2025, with participation from at least 35 businesses representing different parts of the value chain.  
   * Development of at least three pilot projects or collaborative initiatives emerging from workshop discussions.  
2. **Networking and Study Trips:**  
   * Facilitation of at least two chef-farmer networking sessions and one cross-sectoral study trip.  
   * Strengthened relationships between stakeholders, fostering collaboration on circular practices.  
3. **Workshop Outcomes Documentation:**  
   * A documented summary of workshop outcomes, including actionable recommendations, participant feedback, and co-created solutions.

**Verification:**

* Participant attendance logs, feedback forms, and workshop summary reports will validate the engagement of at least 35 businesses and the outcomes achieved.  
* Reports on pilot projects and study trip insights will showcase real-world applications of workshop findings.

---

### **Goal 2: Establish Oslo’s Urban Food System Competence Hub**

**Sub-goal 2.1: Newsletter** **Results:**

1. **Content and Distribution:**  
   * Monthly or bi-monthly newsletters distributed to at least 150 stakeholders by the end of 2025\.  
   * Coverage of at least 10 key topics, including circular food system innovations, global best practices, and project updates.  
2. **Stakeholder Engagement:**  
   * A sustained subscriber engagement rate of 50% or higher, measured through email opens, clicks, and responses.

**Verification:**

* Newsletter analytics (e.g., open and click rates) and a growing subscriber base will verify the success of this activity.  
* Qualitative feedback from subscribers will assess content quality and relevance.

---

**Sub-goal 2.2: Seminars** **Results:**

1. **Seminar Execution:**  
   * Completion of four seminars by 2026, focused on themes such as waste stream integration, circular business models, and policy frameworks.  
   * Engagement of at least 100 participants in total, including industry leaders, policymakers, and local businesses.  
2. **Knowledge Dissemination:**  
   * Documented key takeaways and insights from each seminar, shared with stakeholders to promote knowledge transfer and inspire action.

**Verification:**

* Seminar attendance records, feedback surveys, and documentation of seminar outcomes will confirm participant numbers and the quality of discussions.  
* Distribution of seminar takeaways will further validate knowledge dissemination.

---

**Sub-goal 2.3: Community Engagement** **Results:**

1. **Ecosystem Strengthening:**  
   * Engagement of at least five new community partners (e.g., ByBi, Losæter, Food Studio) to build a stronger circular food ecosystem.  
   * Collaboration on at least three pilot projects showcasing innovative circular solutions (e.g., food waste repurposed for animal feed or packaging for construction).  
2. **Capacity Building and Events:**  
   * Organization of at least three community events focused on circular culture, attended by 150+ participants in total.  
   * Delivery of training and resources to at least 10 community stakeholders to build capacity for circular practices.

**Verification:**

* Signed collaboration agreements with new partners and documented pilot project outcomes will verify progress.  
* Event attendance logs and participant feedback will assess the success of community engagement efforts.

---

### **Evaluation and Monitoring Results**

**Results:**

1. **Progress Tracking:**  
   * Regular updates on project milestones, including mapping completion, workshops, seminars, and community engagement targets.  
   * Detailed metrics on attendance, engagement, and stakeholder feedback across all activities.  
2. **Impact Assessment:**  
   * A comprehensive evaluation report by 2026, detailing the project’s contributions to fostering circularity in Oslo’s food and beverage industry.  
   * Documentation of at least five success stories and lessons learned, highlighting replicable solutions and future opportunities.

**Verification:**

* Monthly or quarterly progress reports and final impact assessments will provide measurable and verifiable insights into the project’s achievements.

### Impacts: (take from effects on problem tree) 

**USE:** *Inputs (Resources) → Activities → Products and Services → User Effects → Societal Effects*  
*Innsatsfaktorer (Inputs):*  
*These refer to the resources or inputs required to implement the project. This includes financial resources, human resources (skills, expertise), materials, technology, and any other necessary support.*  
*Aktiviteter (Activities):*  
*These are the actions or tasks that will be carried out using the inputs. Activities are the concrete steps taken to achieve the project's objectives, such as conducting workshops, creating products, or offering services.*

*Produkter og tjenester (Products and Services):*  
*The outputs of the activities. These are the tangible or intangible items produced during the project, such as reports, prototypes, training materials, or new services offered.*  
*Brukereffekter (User Effects):*  
*These are the direct impacts on the users or beneficiaries of the project’s products or services. This could include skills gained, improved knowledge, behavioral changes, or increased satisfaction among the target audience.*  
*Samfunnseffekter (Societal Effects):*  
*The broader, long-term effects of the project on society as a whole. This can include improvements in social welfare, economic growth, environmental sustainability, or other societal benefits that stem from the user effects, leading to a positive impact on communities, regions, or the nation.*  
 *The results of the project should contribute to achieving its goals. Explain how the results will lead to goal attainment, i.e., what effects the results will produce. Comment on the critical factors for this goal achievement and identify where in the project there is the highest risk of not meeting the goals.*  
*The Directorate for Financial Management (DFØ) has created a guide on results-based management. You can find more information on how projects can be measured here: \[Guide to Results-Based Management\].*  
RIP: De direkte effektene vi har sett hittil er en økt etterspørsel og samarbeidsevne mellom de ulike selskapene. Vi ønsker å bevare denne effekten og samtidig skape forretningsmessige relasjoner som strekker seg utenfor lokalene våre.  
Ved å være inkludert i nærmiljøet skaper vi en merverdi ved vår tilstedeværelse, og det blir enkelt for lokale ildsjeler å komme inn med egne prosjekter, ønsker og vilje. Den energien som ligger i nærkontakten med lokalmiljøet fører til et referansegrunnlag for bedrifter som bidrar i innsalgsprosesser og utviklingen av området generelt.

### **1\. Inputs (Innsatsfaktorer)**

* **Financial Resources:** Funding from various sources to cover activities such as mapping, workshops, seminars, and community engagement initiatives.  
* **Human Resources:** Experts from knowledge institutions (e.g., SINTEF, OsloMet), Nordic Circular Hotspot, local stakeholders, businesses, NGOs, and regulatory bodies.  
* **Technology and Tools:** Use of data-driven tools like smart journey mining for waste stream mapping, predictive analytics, and knowledge-sharing platforms.  
* **Materials and Infrastructure:** Venues for workshops and seminars, communication materials, and digital tools for newsletters and reporting.

---

### **2\. Activities (Aktiviteter)**

* Conduct detailed mapping of local food industry waste streams, resources, and barriers to circularity.  
* Organize five knowledge-sharing workshops, including study trips and chef-farmer networking sessions.  
* Develop and distribute newsletters to communicate findings and promote circular practices.  
* Host four seminars focused on urban food system innovations and best practices.  
* Engage with local stakeholders and build partnerships with at least five new collaborators.  
* Collaborate on pilot projects to demonstrate and test circular solutions.

---

### **3\. Products and Services (Produkter og Tjenester)**

* **Mapping Reports:** Comprehensive reports highlighting waste streams, resources, barriers, and opportunities for circular solutions in Oslo’s food industry.  
* **Workshops and Seminars:** Five workshops and four seminars providing actionable insights, co-created solutions, and networking opportunities.  
* **Knowledge Resources:** Regular newsletters sharing project updates, best practices, and success stories.  
* **Collaborative Pilots:** Pilot projects showcasing circular innovations such as food waste repurposing or resource recovery models.

---

### **4\. User Effects (Brukereffekter)**

* **Increased Knowledge:** Local businesses and stakeholders gain practical knowledge on implementing circular practices, addressing barriers, and utilizing waste as a resource.  
* **Behavioral Changes:** Businesses adopt circular solutions, optimize their waste streams, and engage in cross-sectoral collaboration.  
* **Stronger Networks:** Establishment of cross-sectoral partnerships and knowledge-sharing networks among businesses, regulatory bodies, and community actors.  
* **Enhanced Collaboration:** Chefs, farmers, start-ups, and established businesses collaborate to co-create innovative solutions, resulting in a more integrated food system.  
* **Increased Engagement:** Local stakeholders actively participate in circular initiatives, creating momentum for systemic change in Oslo’s food system.

---

### **5\. Societal Effects (Samfunnseffekter)**

* **Environmental Sustainability:** Reduction in food waste, increased resource recovery, and lowered greenhouse gas emissions from the food system.  
* **Economic Growth:** Creation of circular business opportunities, job creation in waste management and resource recovery, and cost savings for businesses.  
* **Cultural Shift:** Promotion of a circular culture in Oslo’s food industry, encouraging systemic change and sustainable consumption patterns.  
* **Community Empowerment:** Strengthened local ecosystems and increased involvement of community actors, leading to more resilient and self-sustaining food systems.  
* **Global Leadership:** Oslo establishes itself as a leader in circular food systems, serving as a model for other cities.

---

### **Linking Results to Goal Achievement**

The **results** outlined (mapping reports, workshops, seminars, newsletters, and pilot projects) will directly contribute to the project's goals by:

1. Identifying barriers and opportunities for circularity, which provides a roadmap for stakeholders to act.  
2. Facilitating knowledge sharing and collaboration, which will help break down silos and foster innovation.  
3. Engaging stakeholders at all levels, ensuring widespread adoption of circular solutions.

**Critical Success Factors:**

* Active engagement from businesses and regulatory bodies.  
* Successful dissemination of knowledge and actionable insights.  
* Strong leadership in facilitating cross-sectoral collaboration.

**Risks:**

* Resistance to change among stakeholders due to logistical or financial concerns.  
* Insufficient data sharing, limiting the effectiveness of mapping and solution development.  
* Challenges in scaling pilot projects due to regulatory or funding barriers.

### National Goals- 

*To stabilize or increase the population?*  
*To strengthen, secure, or establish jobs?*  
*To maintain/develop existing businesses or contribute to new establishments?*  
*Increased innovation or innovation capacity?*  
*Increased competence (both practical skills and formal education) for the target group?*  
*Increased accessibility (better roads, ports, other transport measures, and broadband)?*  
*To strengthen regional centers?*  
*To make the location/municipality/area more attractive as a place to live or for business location choices?*

### Attachments

[Circular food system arena presentation](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1DR6ANQd9yC_QjaK89aMVPHDvEWlb0Q0eIJ5uA2NWNdY/edit?usp=drive_link)

### BUDGET

#### Krav til budsjett og finansieringsplan

I søknaden skal budsjett (etter kostnadsart eller aktiviteter) og finansieringsplan for prosjektåret presenteres i sin helhet. Finansiering fra samtlige medvirkende aktører, både offentlige og private, skal framgå av søknaden. Det kan kun søkes for året 2025\.

* Minimum 50 % av kostnadene må være dekket av annen finansiering og/eller egeninnsats.

---

#### Overheadkostnader i prosjekter

For teknisk og administrativt personell fra utdannings- og høyskolesektoren som lønnes gjennom prosjektet, skal faktisk lønn (inkludert sosiale kostnader) med et påslag (overheadsats) på 25 % for indirekte kostnader legges til grunn for budsjettering av kostnader.

---

#### Krav til beregning av timesats for egeninnsats

I søknaden skal det oppgis timesatser for deltakerne i prosjektet, samt antall timer det planlegges for.

**Merk:** Ved prosjektrapportering skal det som dokumentasjon av personal- og indirekte kostnader inkluderes timelister for deltakerne i prosjektet, fordelt med:

* Navn på person.  
* Antall timer.  
* Timesats.  
* Hvilke deler av prosjektet som er bearbeidet.

**Beregning av timesats for prosjektmedarbeidere:**

* Timesatsen for egeninnsats beregnes med utgangspunkt i nominell årslønn (brutto lønn) med en maksimalsats på 1,2 ‰.  
* Timesatsen kan likevel ikke overstige kr 1100,-.

**Eksempel på utregning:**

* Johanne har nominell lønn på kr 550 000\. Hun jobber 750 timer med prosjektet i løpet av et år.  
* Beregning:  
  * **Timesats:** 550 000 × 1,2 ‰ \= 660  
  * **Kostnad i prosjektregnskapet:** 660 × 750 \= 495 000

For eksterne deltakere (oppmøte på møter, seminarer og konferanser initiert av prosjektet) kan det brukes en gjennomsnittssats. Maksimalsatsen er 1,2 promille av tenkt gjennomsnittlig bruttolønn og inntil kr 1100,- per time.

**Prosjektansvarlig** er ansvarlig for at de satsene som benyttes sikrer at de samlede timekostnadene ikke overstiger 1,2 promille for de enkelte deltakerne eller 1650 timer per år.

---

#### Krav til søker i forhold til EØS-avtalens regler om offentlig støtte

Støtten fra Innovasjonsprogram Oslo gis med hjemmel i statsstøtteregelverket og bestemmelsene om det alminnelige gruppeunntaket, og rapporteres til ESA i tråd med EØS-avtalens regler om offentlig støtte.

Støtten fra Innovasjonsprogram Oslo kan tildeles innenfor følgende kategorier/gruppeunntak i henhold til EØS-statsstøtteregelverk:

1. **Art. 18: Støtte til rådgivningstjenester for små og mellomstore bedrifter:**  
   * Støtte kan gis for å dekke rådgivningstjenester som ytes av eksterne rådgivere og knyttes til ekstraordinær aktivitet.  
   * Terskelverdiene i artikkel 4 overholdes.  
   * Støtteintensiteten skal ikke overstige 50 % av støtteberettigede kostnader og kan ikke være relatert til drift.  
2. **Art. 25(2)(d): Forundersøkelser:**  
   * Støtte gis under artikkel 25 pkt. 3 (a og e) og dekker kun kostnader til forundersøkelser.  
   * Terskelverdiene i artikkel 4 overholdes.  
   * Støtteintensiteten vil ikke overstige 50 % av støtteberettigede kostnader, men kan økes til:  
     * 60 % for mellomstore bedrifter.  
     * 70 % for små bedrifter (jf. Art. 25 punkt 7).  
3. **Art. 27: Støtte til innovasjonsklynger:**  
   * Støtte gis kun som driftsstøtte til utvikling og gjennomføring av aktiviteter i innovasjonsklynger over en periode på maks 10 år.  
   * Støtte gis utelukkende til klyngeorganisasjonen.  
   * Støtteberettigede kostnader knyttes til personell- og administrative kostnader som beskrevet i artikkel 27 pkt. 8 (a-c).  
   * Støtteintensiteten skal ikke overstige 50 % av støtteberettigede kostnader.  
4. **Art. 28(2)(c): Innovasjonsstøtte til små og mellomstore bedrifter:**  
   * Støtte gis kun til kostnader for rådgivnings- og støttetjenester knyttet til innovasjon.  
   * Terskelverdiene i artikkel 4 overholdes.  
   * Støtteintensiteten skal ikke overstige 50 %, men kan økes til 100 % dersom det samlede støttebeløpet for slike tjenester ikke overstiger €200 000 per bedrift over en treårsperiode.  
5. **Art. 29: Støtte til prosess- og organisasjonsinnovasjon:**  
   * Støtte gis kun til kostnader beskrevet i artikkel 29 pkt. 3 (a, b og d).  
   * For store foretak kreves det samarbeid med SMB-er som står for minst 30 % av de støtteberettigede kostnadene.  
   * Maks støtteintensitet:  
     * 15 % for store foretak.  
     * 50 % for SMB-er (35 % ved samarbeid med store foretak).  
6. **Art. 31: Støtte til opplæring:**  
   * Støtte gis kun i henhold til artikkel 31 pkt. 3 (a-c).  
   * Støtte til opplæring som er obligatorisk etter nasjonale standarder, er ikke tillatt.  
   * Maks støtteintensitet:  
     * 50 % av støtteberettigede kostnader.  
     * Økes til:  
       * 60 % for mellomstore bedrifter.  
       * 70 % for små bedrifter.

**Søker må redegjøre for andre tilskuddsordninger som vurderes søkt, og hvordan midlene fra Innovasjonsprogram Oslo skal komplementere denne støtten.**

**Merk:**

* Støtte fra programmet kan ikke kombineres med annen offentlig finansiering for de samme kostnadene.  
* Det skal heller ikke resultere i en støtteintensitet som overskrider reguleringene.

---

#### Støtteintensitet

* Støtteandelen fra Innovasjonsprogram Oslo skal som hovedregel ikke overstige 50 % av prosjektets totalbudsjett.  
* Som regel dekkes det ikke mer enn kr 1 000 000 per prosjekt per år.

| Opplysninger om mottatt offentlig støtte |
| :---- |
|  **Har du mottatt offentlig støtte de siste tre årene, eller har du sendt søknad om offentlig støtte som du ennå ikke har fått svar på?**  						 **Hvis ja, hva slags offentlig støtte har du mottatt eller søkt om? (\* Obligatorisk felt ved svar Ja på første felt.)**  **Tidsplan** Prosjektet skal ha en realistisk milepælsplan hvor hovedaktivitetene er tidfestet.Eksempel:2013 Mai \- Planlegging ferdig2013 Juni \- Prosjektgruppe etablert2013 4.kvartal \- Risikoanalyse produsert og konklusjon for videre arbeid besluttet2014 1.mai \- sluttrapport og prosjekt fullført   |

[^1]:  [Cities-and-Circular-Economy-for-Food.pdf](https://pacecircular.org/sites/default/files/2019-03/Cities-and-Circular-Economy-for-Food.pdf)

[^2]:  [Cities-and-Circular-Economy-for-Food.pdf](https://pacecircular.org/sites/default/files/2019-03/Cities-and-Circular-Economy-for-Food.pdf)

[^3]:  [EAT-Cities-2022.pdf](https://eatforum.org/content/uploads/2022/05/EAT-Cities-2022.pdf)
